q: Which cranial nerve is completely decussated, innervating the contralateral superior oblique?
w: CN4 is the only fully crossed cranial nerve; it supplies the contralateral superior oblique.
k: CN4 = only fully decussated; contralateral SO.

q: A pupil-involving CN3 palsy (ptosis, eye down-and-out, dilated fixed pupil) is concerning for:
w: Pupillomotor fibers run on the outside of CN3, so external compression (aneurysm) involves the pupil early. A surgical emergency.
k: Pupil-involving CN3 = compressive aneurysm until excluded.

q: In CN3 palsy, an ischemic (diabetic) cause classically:
w: Ischemia hits the inner motor fibers first and spares the outer pupillomotor fibers, so the pupil is spared.
k: Compressive = pupil blown; ischemic = pupil spared.

q: Corneal sensation is carried by which nerve?
w: The ophthalmic division (V1) of the trigeminal, via the nasociliary nerve, provides corneal sensation.
k: Corneal sensation = CN5 V1 (nasociliary).

q: Which division of the trigeminal nerve is the smallest and purely sensory?
w: V1 (ophthalmic) is the smallest and purely sensory; V3 (mandibular) is the largest and mixed.
k: V1 = smallest, pure sensory; V3 = largest.

q: Orbital cellulitis most commonly spreads from which sinus?
w: The thin lamina papyracea of the ethmoid shares the medial orbital wall, so ethmoiditis is the usual source.
k: Ethmoidal sinus → orbital cellulitis.

q: Sphenoidal sinusitis is classically associated with which ocular complication?
w: The optic nerve runs above the sphenoid sinus, so sphenoiditis causes retrobulbar neuritis, not orbital cellulitis.
k: Sphenoidal sinus → retrobulbar neuritis (CN2).

q: Which cranial nerve mediates both eyelid closure and reflex lacrimal secretion?
w: CN7 closes the lids (orbicularis) and drives reflex tearing via the greater petrosal nerve.
k: CN7 = lid closure + reflex tearing.

q: The optic nerve (CN2) and ophthalmic artery enter the orbit through the:
w: CN2 and the ophthalmic artery pass through the optic canal (lesser wing of sphenoid), not the SOF.
k: CN2 + ophthalmic artery = optic canal.

q: Which cranial nerve has the longest intracranial course?
w: CN4 has the longest intracranial course (~75 mm) and is also the thinnest motor nerve.
k: CN4 = longest intracranial course.

q: The lateral rectus is innervated by:
w: By the rule LR6 SO4 AO3: lateral rectus = CN6, superior oblique = CN4, all others = CN3.
k: LR6 SO4 AO3.

q: The primary visual cortex (area 17) is located in which lobe?
w: Area 17 (primary visual cortex) sits in the medial occipital lobe.
k: Area 17 = occipital lobe.

q: What is the PRIMARY action of the superior oblique muscle?
w: The SO's primary action is intorsion; depression is secondary (and abduction tertiary).
k: SO primary action = intorsion.

q: Which extraocular muscle does NOT originate from the annulus of Zinn?
w: The inferior oblique originates from the maxilla (orbital floor); all other EOMs arise from the annulus of Zinn.
k: IO = only EOM not from annulus of Zinn.

q: The thinnest (weakest) orbital wall is the:
w: The medial wall (lamina papyracea of the ethmoid) is thinnest; the lateral wall is thickest.
k: Medial wall thinnest; lateral thickest.

q: The superior orbital fissure transmits all EXCEPT:
w: The SOF carries CN3, CN4, CN6, V1 branches, and the superior ophthalmic vein. CN2 goes through the optic canal.
k: CN2 = optic canal, not SOF.

q: Which rectus muscle inserts NEAREST the limbus and is the strongest?
w: By the Spiral of Tillaux, the MR inserts nearest (5.5 mm) and is the strongest; SR inserts farthest (7.7 mm).
k: MR nearest (5.5 mm, strongest).

q: The longest extraocular muscle is the:
w: The superior oblique, including its tendon, is the longest EOM at ~60 mm.
k: SO = longest EOM (60 mm).

q: A patient has vertical diplopia worse on downgaze and adduction, with a compensatory head tilt. The palsy is:
w: CN4/SO palsy gives vertical diplopia worst down-and-in, with a Bielschowsky head tilt to the opposite shoulder.
k: CN4 palsy = vertical diplopia + head tilt.

q: How do the rectus and oblique muscles insert relative to the equator?
o: Both in front of the equator
Recti in front, obliques behind the equator
w: The recti insert anterior to the equator; the obliques insert posterior to it.
k: Recti front of equator; obliques behind.

q: Removal of the eye's internal contents while preserving the sclera and EOMs is called:
w: Evisceration removes only the internal contents (sclera stays). Enucleation removes the whole globe.
k: Evisceration = contents only; enucleation = whole globe.

q: The suspensory ligament of the eyeball (a thickening of Tenon's capsule) is:
w: Lockwood's ligament (with IR and IO fascia) forms the suspensory hammock of the globe.
k: Lockwood's = suspensory ligament of the eye.

q: How many bones form the bony orbit?
w: Seven bones: maxilla, frontal, sphenoid, zygomatic, lacrimal, palatine, ethmoid.
k: 7 orbital bones.

q: The orbital septum is clinically important because it:
w: The septum separates preseptal (anterior, milder) from orbital (posterior, sight-threatening) infection.
k: Septum = preseptal vs orbital divide.

q: From outside in toward the cornea, the tear film layers are:
w: Outer lipid (anti-evaporative) → aqueous (bulk) → mucin (wets the cornea).
k: Lipid → aqueous → mucin.

q: Which is the thickest layer of the tear film?
w: Aqueous (~7 µm) is thickest; lipid (~0.5 µm) and mucin (~0.03 µm) are far thinner.
k: Aqueous is thickest (~7 µm).

q: The basic (resting) aqueous tear secretion comes from:
w: Krause and Wolfring provide basic aqueous; the main lacrimal gland is for reflex secretion (CN7).
k: Basic aqueous = Krause + Wolfring.

q: Schirmer's test measures, and TBUT measures, respectively:
w: Schirmer = aqueous volume (normal 10–25 mm/5 min); TBUT = stability (normal >10 s).
k: Schirmer = volume; TBUT = stability.

q: A patient has Schirmer's 18 mm but TBUT of 5 seconds. The likely problem is:
w: Normal aqueous volume with a short break-up time points to lipid-layer deficiency (MGD), i.e., evaporative dry eye.
k: Normal Schirmer + short TBUT = evaporative (MGD).

q: The nasolacrimal duct drains into the:
w: The NLD opens into the inferior meatus, guarded by the valve of Hasner.
k: NLD → inferior meatus (valve of Hasner).

q: Horner's syndrome produces what kind of ptosis?
w: Horner's paralyzes Müller's muscle, giving a mild 1–2 mm ptosis. Complete ptosis needs LPS (CN3) failure.
k: Müller's paralysis = 1–2 mm partial ptosis.

q: The predominant immunoglobulin in tears is:
w: Secretory IgA is the major tear immunoglobulin, with lysozyme and lactoferrin for defense.
k: Tears = secretory IgA.

q: 'Crocodile tears' (gustatory lacrimation) result from:
o: A tumor of the lacrimal gland
w: After a CN7 lesion, regenerating salivary fibers misroute to the lacrimal gland, so eating triggers tearing.
k: Crocodile tears = misdirected CN7 fibers.

q: Goblet cells, which secrete mucin, are absent at the:
w: Goblet cells are densest at the fornix and absent at the lid margins and limbus.
k: Goblet cells absent at lid margins + limbus.

q: A 6-week-old with unilateral epiphora and mucopurulent discharge since birth most likely has:
w: A non-canalized NLD (failure at the valve of Hasner) causes stasis, infection, and chronic discharge.
k: Infant epiphora + discharge = NLD obstruction/dacryocystitis.

q: The cornea contributes about how much refractive power, making it the strongest lens of the eye?
w: The cornea provides ~43 D, the most of any ocular refracting surface.
k: Cornea ~43 D (strongest refractor).

q: Which corneal layer makes up about 90% of corneal thickness?
w: The stroma is ~90% of corneal thickness; the epithelium is ~10% of weight.
k: Stroma = 90% of thickness.

q: Which corneal layer CANNOT regenerate after injury?
w: Bowman's (and the endothelium) cannot regenerate; epithelium and Descemet's can.
k: Bowman's + endothelium do not regenerate.

q: Corneal thickness is:
w: The cornea is thinner centrally (~0.52–0.56 mm) and thickens toward the periphery (0.7–1.0 mm).
k: Cornea thinner centrally.

q: Corneal deturgescence (clarity) is maintained by:
w: The endothelial Na-K ATPase pumps ions and draws water out of the stroma, keeping it clear.
k: Endothelial Na-K ATPase = deturgescence.

q: Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy causes corneal edema because:
o: Endothelial cell loss disables the pump
w: Endothelium is post-mitotic; once cell density falls too low, the pump fails and the stroma swells.
k: Fuchs' = endothelial loss → pump failure → edema.

q: The sclera is thickest at which point?
w: The sclera is thickest (~1.0 mm) at the CN2 entrance and thinnest (~0.3 mm) under the EOM insertions.
k: Sclera thickest at CN2; thinnest at EOM insertion.

q: Why is the cornea transparent but the sclera white?
o: The sclera is much thicker than cornea
The cornea is more vascular than sclera
w: Uniform corneal collagen spacing transmits light; the sclera's irregular, interlacing fibers scatter it (opaque).
k: Regular collagen = clear; irregular = white.

q: The cornea is innervated by the nasociliary nerve in all layers EXCEPT:
w: Sensory innervation reaches all layers except Descemet's and the endothelium.
k: All corneal layers innervated except Descemet's/endothelium.

q: A descemetocele is:
o: An ectasia of the anterior sclera
A defect of the corneal epithelium
w: In a descemetocele, the stroma has perforated but the elastic Descemet's membrane remains intact and bulges forward.
k: Descemetocele = intact Descemet's bulging through stroma.

q: On gonioscopy, the most anterior landmark, a glistening line at the end of Descemet's, is:
w: Schwalbe's line marks the termination of Descemet's membrane, the most anterior angle landmark.
k: Schwalbe's line = most anterior angle landmark.

q: The horizontal corneal diameter relative to the vertical is:
w: The cornea is oval, wider horizontally (~11–12 mm) than vertically (~10–11 mm).
k: Cornea oval, wider horizontally.

q: The uveal tract consists of:
w: The uvea is the vascular middle coat: iris, ciliary body, and choroid.
k: Uvea = iris + ciliary body + choroid.

q: During accommodation, the ciliary muscle contracts and:
o: Tightens the zonules, flattening the lens
Has no effect on the lens
Relaxes the zonules, letting the lens round up
w: Ciliary contraction reduces the ring diameter, slackening zonules so the elastic lens rounds up (more power).
k: Ciliary contracts → zonules relax → lens rounds.

q: The preferred surgical entry into the eye for vitreoretinal procedures is the:
w: The pars plana is the least vascular ciliary body zone, minimizing bleeding on entry.
k: Pars plana = safe surgical entry.

q: The sphincter pupillae is innervated by:
w: The sphincter (miosis) is parasympathetic (CN3 via the ciliary ganglion); the dilator is sympathetic.
k: Sphincter = parasympathetic CN3.

q: The choroid is thickest at the:
w: The choroid is thickest at the posterior pole and thinnest anteriorly.
k: Choroid thickest at posterior pole.

q: Bilateral small, irregular pupils that constrict to near but not to light indicate:
w: Light-near dissociation with small irregular pupils is Argyll-Robertson (neurosyphilis, pretectal lesion).
k: Argyll-Robertson = light-near dissociation, neurosyphilis.

q: An Adie's tonic pupil is caused by a lesion at the:
w: Adie's results from ciliary ganglion damage; it constricts to dilute (0.125%) pilocarpine from denervation supersensitivity.
k: Adie's = ciliary ganglion (benign).

q: Dilute 0.125% pilocarpine constricts which pupil?
w: Normal pupils do not respond to this low dose; an Adie's pupil constricts due to supersensitivity.
k: 0.125% pilocarpine constricts Adie's (not normal).

q: The dilator pupillae lies in which zone of the iris?
w: The radial dilator occupies the peripheral (ciliary) zone; the circular sphincter sits in the central pupillary zone.
k: Dilator = ciliary zone; sphincter = pupillary zone.

q: The choriocapillaris supplies which part of the retina?
w: The choriocapillaris feeds the outer retina (65–85% of retinal blood flow); the central retinal artery feeds the inner layers.
k: Choriocapillaris = outer retina.

q: The layer between the choroid and the RPE is:
w: Bruch's membrane (~7 µm) separates the choriocapillaris from the RPE; it is involved in ARMD (drusen).
k: Bruch's membrane sits between choroid and RPE.

q: The normal resting pupil diameter is about:
w: Normal pupil size is ~3–4 mm; <1.5 mm is miotic and >5 mm is mydriatic.
k: Normal pupil 3–4 mm.

q: Aqueous humor is produced by the:
w: The non-pigmented ciliary epithelium of the pars plicata secretes aqueous at ~2 µL/min.
k: Aqueous from non-pigment ciliary epithelium.

q: What percentage of aqueous outflow is via the trabecular meshwork?
w: ~80% drains through the trabecular (conventional) route; ~20% via uveoscleral. Prostaglandins boost uveoscleral.
k: Trabecular 80%, uveoscleral 20%.

q: Which lens capsule region is thickest (and the thickest basement membrane in the body)?
w: The anterior capsule (~15 µm) is the thickest BM in the body; the posterior (~2.2 µm) is thinnest.
k: Anterior capsule thickest; posterior thinnest.

q: The primary mechanism of presbyopia is:
w: Lifelong fiber compaction hardens the nucleus so it cannot round up even when zonular tension is released.
k: Presbyopia = lens nuclear hardening.

q: The vitreous gel is approximately:
w: Vitreous is ~98.5% water; hyaluronic acid gives it its gel character. Volume ~4.5 mL.
k: Vitreous ~98.5% water (hyaluronic acid).

q: Compared with plasma, aqueous humor has:
w: Aqueous is protein-poor but has a much higher ascorbate concentration than plasma (antioxidant role).
k: Aqueous = high ascorbate, low protein.

q: Normal intraocular pressure ranges from:
w: Normal IOP is 10–21 mmHg; episcleral venous pressure (~10 mmHg) sets the floor.
k: Normal IOP 10–21 mmHg.

q: How does aqueous humor differ from vitreous?
o: Vitreous is made by the ciliary body
Aqueous is replaced; vitreous is not
w: Aqueous is continuously produced and drained; vitreous gel is developmental and not replaced if lost.
k: Aqueous flows; vitreous gel is not replaced.

q: The remnant of the embryonic hyaloid artery passing through the vitreous is:
w: Cloquet's canal is the hyaloid remnant, ending at the area of Martegiani near the disc.
k: Cloquet's canal = hyaloid remnant.

q: Which class of glaucoma drug lowers IOP mainly by increasing uveoscleral outflow?
w: Prostaglandins increase the uveoscleral (~20%) outflow pathway; the others mostly cut production.
k: Prostaglandins boost uveoscleral outflow.

q: The posterior lens surface, compared to the anterior, is:
w: The posterior surface (radius ~6 mm) is more curved than the anterior (~10 mm), giving it more power.
k: Posterior lens surface more curved (steeper).

q: Why is the crystalline lens avascular?
o: It is too small for vessels
Vessels would obstruct the optical path
w: Any vasculature would scatter light and blur the image, so the lens relies on aqueous for nutrition.
k: Lens avascular to stay optically clear.

q: A retinal detachment separates which two layers?
w: Detachment occurs at the potential space between the neurosensory photoreceptors and the RPE.
k: RD plane = photoreceptors vs RPE.

q: The foveola contains:
o: Equal numbers of rods and cones
w: The foveola is pure cones, rod-free and capillary-free, giving peak acuity.
k: Foveola = pure cones, capillary-free.

q: Which retinal region is THICKEST?
w: The parafovea is thickest (piled-up displaced ganglion cells); the foveola is the thinnest retina.
k: Parafovea thickest; foveola thinnest.

q: The physiologic blind spot corresponds to the:
w: The optic disc has no photoreceptors, producing the physiologic blind spot.
k: Optic disc = blind spot (no photoreceptors).

q: A normal cup-to-disc ratio is:
w: Normal C/D is ≤0.3; 0.5 is suspicious and 0.8 is highly concerning for glaucoma.
k: Normal C/D ≤0.3.

q: Retinal diseases typically present with painless vision loss because:
o: The retina has no pain fibers
w: The retina lacks pain innervation, so disease is painless and often detected late.
k: Retina has no pain fibers.

q: The inner retinal layers are supplied by:
w: The central retinal artery supplies the inner retina; the choriocapillaris supplies the outer retina.
k: Inner retina = central retinal artery.

q: The macular pigments that filter blue light (and are targeted by AREDS2) are:
w: Lutein and zeaxanthin (xanthophylls) concentrate in the macula and are protective antioxidants.
k: Macular pigment = lutein + zeaxanthin.

q: The ora serrata, the anterior termination of the retina, lies about:
w: The ora serrata is ~6 mm from the limbus, the junction of retina and pars plana.
k: Ora serrata ~6 mm from limbus.

q: In glaucoma, which cells are primarily damaged at the lamina cribrosa?
w: Elevated IOP compresses retinal ganglion cell axons at the lamina cribrosa, causing field loss and cupping.
k: Glaucoma damages RGC axons at the lamina cribrosa.

q: The optic disc is located approximately:
o: Just anterior to the ora serrata
w: The disc sits ~3 mm nasal to the macula and ~0.8 mm above the foveola.
k: Disc ~3 mm nasal to macula.

q: How many layers does the retina have, counting from RPE to the inner limiting membrane?
w: Eleven layers: RPE, photoreceptors, ELM, ONL, Henle, OPL, INL, IPL, GCL, NFL, ILM.
k: Retina = 11 layers.

q: A complete optic nerve lesion causes:
w: Before the chiasm, an optic nerve lesion blinds the entire same-side eye.
k: Optic nerve lesion = ipsilateral blindness.

q: Which fibers cross at the optic chiasm?
w: Nasal retinal fibers decussate; temporal fibers stay ipsilateral. Chiasm damage gives bitemporal loss.
k: Nasal fibers cross at the chiasm.

q: Bitemporal hemianopia is most commonly caused by:
w: A pituitary adenoma pushes up on the chiasm, damaging crossing nasal fibers and losing both temporal fields.
k: Bitemporal hemianopia = chiasm (pituitary).

q: An optic tract lesion produces:
w: Tract lesions give incongruous homonymous hemianopia; Wernicke's hemianopic pupil can occur here.
k: Optic tract = incongruous HH + Wernicke's pupil.

q: A temporal lobe (Meyer's loop) lesion causes:
w: Inferior retinal fibers loop through the temporal lobe; damage gives a contralateral superior quadrant defect.
k: Meyer's loop = 'pie in the sky' (superior).

q: A parietal radiation lesion causes:
w: Superior retinal fibers run through the parietal lobe; damage produces an inferior quadrantanopia.
k: Parietal lesion = inferior quadrantanopia.

q: As lesions move more posteriorly along the pathway, the homonymous defects become:
w: Congruity increases posteriorly: tract lesions are incongruous; occipital lesions are highly congruous.
k: Posterior = more congruous.

q: An occipital cortex lesion characteristically produces a homonymous hemianopia with:
w: Occipital lesions often spare the macula (dual blood supply / large cortical representation).
k: Occipital lesion = macular sparing.

q: Why do cortical (post-LGB) lesions NOT affect the pupillary light reflex?
o: The pupil has a separate retina
Pupil fibers leave at the pretectum
Cortical lesions are mild by nature
The cortex drives the light reflex
w: Pupillomotor fibers leave the tract at the pretectal area, so cortical lesions spare the light reflex.
k: Pupil fibers exit at pretectum (pre-cortex).

q: Binasal hemianopia results from damage to:
o: Meyer's loop in the temporal lobe
w: Lateral compression of the uncrossed temporal fibers on both sides gives a binasal field loss (rare).
k: Binasal = uncrossed temporal fiber damage.

q: At the LGB, temporal (uncrossed) retinal fibers terminate in which layers?
w: Temporal/ipsilateral fibers go to LGB layers 2, 3, 5; nasal/crossed go to 1, 4, 6.
k: Temporal fibers → LGB 2, 3, 5.

q: The white band of myelinated fibers within area 17 is the:
w: The striae of Gennari (geniculocalcarine fibers) give the primary visual cortex its 'striate' name.
k: Striae of Gennari = area 17 marker.

q: The optic vesicle arises from the:
w: The optic vesicle evaginates from the diencephalon; it then induces the surface ectoderm to form the lens.
k: Optic vesicle = diencephalon.

q: Which major ocular structure is derived entirely from surface ectoderm?
w: The lens forms entirely from the surface-ectoderm lens placode; the cornea epithelium also comes from surface ectoderm.
k: Lens = surface ectoderm (entirely).

q: Failure of the fetal (choroidal) fissure to close results in a coloboma located:
w: The fetal fissure lies inferonasally, so colobomas are always inferior (6 o'clock) when it fails to close (~week 7).
k: Coloboma = inferior (fetal fissure).

q: Which vitreous becomes the adult vitreous gel?
w: The secondary (avascular) vitreous becomes the adult gel; the primary regresses; the tertiary becomes zonules.
k: Secondary vitreous = adult gel.

q: The tertiary vitreous gives rise to the:
w: The tertiary vitreous forms the fibrillin-rich zonular fibers that suspend the lens.
k: Tertiary vitreous = lens zonules.

q: Persistent hyperplastic primary vitreous (PHPV/PFV) presents with leukocoria because:
o: The primary vitreous fails to regress
The lens fails to form at all
w: Retained fibrovascular primary vitreous behind the lens appears as a white pupil; absence of calcium helps distinguish it from retinoblastoma.
k: PHPV = primary vitreous fails to regress → leukocoria.

q: In Marfan syndrome (FBN1 mutation), the lens typically dislocates:
w: Weak fibrillin zonules in Marfan cause superotemporal lens dislocation; homocystinuria dislocates inferonasally.
k: Marfan = superior lens dislocation.

q: Aniridia is most associated with a mutation in which gene?
w: PAX6 is a master eye-development gene; its mutation causes aniridia.
k: Aniridia = PAX6 mutation.

q: The corneal stroma and endothelium are derived from:
w: Neural crest cells form the corneal stroma and endothelium; the corneal epithelium comes from surface ectoderm.
k: Corneal stroma + endothelium = neural crest.

q: The retina develops from the:
w: The two walls of the optic cup (neuroectoderm) form the neurosensory retina and the RPE.
k: Retina = neuroectoderm (optic cup).

q: The extraocular muscles are derived from:
w: The EOMs arise from mesoderm; most other orbital connective tissue is neural crest.
k: EOMs = mesoderm.

q: The hyaloid artery normally fully regresses by:
w: The hyaloid system regresses by about week 36 (birth); its canal remnant is Cloquet's canal, and the Mittendorf dot is a posterior-capsule remnant.
k: Hyaloid artery regresses by birth (~week 36).

q: The thinnest motor cranial nerve is:
w: CN4 is both the thinnest motor cranial nerve and the one with the longest intracranial course, which is why it is so vulnerable to closed head trauma.
k: CN4 = thinnest motor nerve, longest course.

q: CN7 exits the skull through which foramen?
w: The facial nerve leaves the skull at the stylomastoid foramen before branching over the face to supply the orbicularis oculi.
k: CN7 exits at the stylomastoid foramen.

q: Besides CN2, the optic canal also transmits the:
w: The optic canal carries CN2, the ophthalmic artery, and sympathetic fibers. The superior ophthalmic vein and the V1 branches travel through the superior orbital fissure instead.
k: Optic canal = CN2 + ophthalmic artery + sympathetics.

q: How many paired bones make up the skull?
w: Eight paired bones: palatine, parietal, temporal, inferior nasal concha, maxilla, nasal, zygoma, and lacrimal.
k: 8 paired skull bones.

q: Which cranial nerve supplies the superior oblique of the opposite side?
w: CN4 decussates completely in the anterior medullary velum, so each trochlear nucleus drives the contralateral superior oblique.
k: CN4 = crossed, supplies contralateral SO.

q: The thickest wall of the orbit is the:
w: The lateral wall, formed by the greater wing of the sphenoid and the zygomatic bone, is the thickest and strongest. The medial wall is the thinnest.
k: Lateral wall thickest; medial thinnest.

q: The primary action of the inferior oblique is:
w: The IO's primary action is extorsion, with elevation secondary and abduction tertiary. The SO is its mirror image: intorsion primary, depression secondary.
k: IO primary = extorsion; SO primary = intorsion.

q: Which rectus muscle inserts farthest from the limbus?
w: By the Spiral of Tillaux the SR inserts farthest at 7.7 mm; the MR is nearest at 5.5 mm and is the strongest rectus.
k: SR farthest (7.7 mm); MR nearest (5.5 mm).

q: The three layers of orbital fascia, from outside in, are:
w: Periorbita lines the bone, the orbital septum forms the anterior barrier, and Tenon's capsule envelops the globe.
k: Periorbita → septum → Tenon's.

q: To prevent sympathetic ophthalmia, a severely traumatized blind eye should be enucleated within:
w: Enucleating the injured eye within about 2 weeks removes the uveal antigen before the fellow eye can mount granulomatous uveitis.
k: Enucleate within 2 weeks to prevent sympathetic ophthalmia.

q: The superior ophthalmic vein enters the orbit through the:
w: The SOF transmits CN3, CN4, CN6, the V1 branches (frontal, lacrimal, nasociliary), and the superior ophthalmic vein.
k: Superior ophthalmic vein = SOF.

q: The dilator pupillae is innervated by:
w: The dilator is sympathetic; the sphincter is parasympathetic via CN3 and the short ciliary nerves. Losing the sympathetic supply gives the miosis of Horner's.
k: Dilator = sympathetic; sphincter = CN3.

q: The safest surgical entry point into the eye through the ciliary body is the:
w: The pars plana is the least vascular part of the ciliary body, so entry there minimizes bleeding. The pars plicata is where aqueous is made and is highly vascular.
k: Pars plana = least vascular = surgical entry.

q: Which layer supplies the outer retina and carries most of the eye's blood flow?
w: The choriocapillaris carries 65–85% of ocular blood flow and nourishes the outer retina, including the photoreceptors. The central retinal artery serves only the inner retina.
k: Choriocapillaris = outer retina, most blood flow.

q: The layer lying between the choroid and the RPE is:
w: Bruch's membrane is about 7 µm thick and separates the choriocapillaris from the RPE. Breaks in it allow choroidal neovascularization in wet AMD.
k: Bruch's membrane sits between choroid and RPE.

q: Argyll-Robertson pupils are classically caused by:
w: Argyll-Robertson pupils come from a pretectal lesion in neurosyphilis or tabes dorsalis: small, irregular pupils that accommodate but do not react to light.
k: Argyll-Robertson = neurosyphilis, pretectal.

q: Adie's tonic pupil results from damage to the:
w: Adie's follows damage to the ciliary ganglion or short ciliary nerves. It is benign, and the denervated sphincter becomes supersensitive to dilute pilocarpine.
k: Adie's = ciliary ganglion, benign.

q: The rate of aqueous humor production is about:
w: Aqueous is produced at roughly 2 µL/min and turns over the anterior chamber about every 100 minutes.
k: Aqueous production = 2 µL/min.

q: What proportion of aqueous leaves by the trabecular route?
w: About 80% drains conventionally through the trabecular meshwork into Schlemm's canal; the remaining 20% leaves by the uveoscleral route, which prostaglandin drugs exploit.
k: Trabecular 80%; uveoscleral 20%.

q: Episcleral venous pressure sets the floor for IOP at about:
w: Aqueous drains into the episcleral veins, so IOP cannot fall below their pressure of roughly 10 mmHg. Normal IOP runs 10–21 mmHg.
k: Episcleral venous pressure ~10 mmHg = IOP floor.

q: The volume of the vitreous is approximately:
w: The vitreous is about 4.5 mL, roughly 80% of the globe's volume, and is 98.5% water held as a gel by hyaluronic acid.
k: Vitreous = 4.5 mL, 98.5% water.

q: Which molecule gives the vitreous its gel character?
w: Hyaluronic acid binds water and holds the vitreous as a gel. As it degrades with age the gel liquefies, producing syneresis and posterior vitreous detachment.
k: Hyaluronic acid = vitreous gel.

q: The thickest basement membrane in the human body is the:
w: The anterior lens capsule is about 15 µm thick, the thickest basement membrane in the body. The posterior capsule is the thinnest at 2.2 µm, which is why it is so easily ruptured in surgery.
k: Anterior lens capsule = thickest BM (15 µm).

q: At rest, the crystalline lens contributes approximately how much refractive power?
w: The unaccommodated lens supplies 10–15 D. The cornea is the stronger element at about 43 D, but only the lens can change its power.
k: Lens at rest = 10–15 D; cornea = 43 D.

q: Cloquet's canal is a remnant of the:
w: Cloquet's (the hyaloid) canal runs from the disc to the posterior lens, marking the path of the regressed hyaloid artery. Its dilated end at the disc is the area of Martegiani.
k: Cloquet's canal = hyaloid artery remnant.

q: Muscae volitantes (floaters) represent:
w: Muscae volitantes are vitreous floaters left over from the embryonic hyaloid vascular system, seen as drifting specks against a bright background.
k: Muscae volitantes = hyaloid remnants.

q: The crystalline lens is avascular because:
o: Vessels would obstruct the optical path
Blood vessels cannot cross a capsule
w: Any vessel crossing the lens would scatter light and block the visual axis, so the lens is nourished entirely by aqueous instead.
k: Lens avascular to keep the optical path clear.

q: During accommodation, the ciliary muscle:
w: Ciliary contraction pulls the ring forward and inward, which reduces its diameter, slackens the zonules, and lets the elastic lens round up. This is the step presbyopia defeats.
k: Ciliary contracts → zonules slacken → lens rounds.

q: The sphincter pupillae lies in which zone of the iris?
w: The sphincter sits centrally in the pupillary zone; the dilator lies peripherally in the ciliary zone. The collarette marks the boundary between the two.
k: Sphincter = pupillary zone; dilator = ciliary zone.

q: The choroid is thinnest at which location?
w: The choroid runs about 0.25 mm at the posterior pole and thins to roughly 0.10 mm anteriorly.
k: Choroid: 0.25 mm posteriorly, 0.10 mm anteriorly.

q: Which pupil abnormality is a benign peripheral neuropathy?
w: Adie's is a benign peripheral lesion of the ciliary ganglion. Argyll-Robertson is central, from neurosyphilis and tabes dorsalis, and is anything but benign.
k: Adie's benign peripheral; Argyll-Robertson central syphilis.

q: Intraocular pressure is maintained principally by:
w: IOP is set by the balance of aqueous production against outflow. The vitreous contributes to globe shape but plays no part in IOP dynamics.
k: IOP = aqueous production vs outflow, not vitreous.

q: Comparing the lens surfaces, the posterior surface is:
w: The posterior lens surface has a 6 mm radius and the anterior 10 mm, so the posterior is steeper and contributes more refractive power. Greater curvature means more power.
k: Posterior lens 6 mm (steeper); anterior 10 mm.

q: The THICKEST part of the retina is the:
w: The parafovea is thickest because ganglion cells are displaced there, piled aside to clear the foveal light path. The foveola itself is the thinnest retina.
k: Parafovea thickest; foveola thinnest.

q: In a retinal detachment, the separation occurs between the:
w: Detachment splits the neurosensory retina from the RPE at the old optic-cup potential space. The RPE-Bruch's bond is firm and does not give way.
k: RD separates photoreceptors from RPE.

q: Retinal disease characteristically presents as painless vision loss because the retina has no:
w: The retina carries no nociceptive fibers, so disease progresses without warning pain. That silence is exactly why retinal disease is so often caught late.
k: Retina has no pain fibers = painless loss.

q: The inner retinal layers are supplied by the:
w: The CRA and its four branches feed the inner retina; the choriocapillaris supplies only the outer retina, the RPE and photoreceptors. This split is why a CRAO spares the fovea via the cilioretinal artery in some eyes.
k: CRA = inner retina; choriocapillaris = outer retina.

q: Congruity of a homonymous field defect becomes complete:
w: Congruity increases the further back the lesion. Optic tract lesions are post-chiasmal yet incongruous; only at the LGB and beyond do the defects match exactly.
k: The further back the lesion, the more congruous.

q: A patient with bitemporal hemianopia retains:
w: Bitemporal hemianopia knocks out the temporal fields but preserves nasal fields and central vision, so acuity can stay 20/20 while the patient collides with door frames.
k: Bitemporal hemianopia spares nasal + central vision.

q: Wernicke's hemianopic pupil occurs only with a lesion of the:
w: Wernicke's pupil belongs to optic tract lesions. Cortical lesions spare the light reflex entirely, because pupillary fibers leave at the pretectum before the cortex.
k: Wernicke's pupil = optic tract lesion only.

q: The crystalline lens derives from:
w: The lens comes entirely from surface ectoderm via the lens placode, the only major ocular structure with that origin. The retina is neuroectoderm; the corneal stroma and endothelium are neural crest.
k: Lens = surface ectoderm (unique).

q: The corneal stroma and endothelium derive from:
w: Neural crest cells give rise to the corneal stroma and endothelium, plus the trabecular meshwork and much of the sclera. Only the corneal epithelium is surface ectoderm.
k: Corneal stroma + endothelium = neural crest.

q: The adult vitreous gel develops from the:
w: The secondary vitreous becomes the adult gel. The primary vitreous regresses, leaving only Cloquet's canal, and the tertiary vitreous becomes the lens zonules.
k: Secondary = adult gel; tertiary = zonules; primary = Cloquet's.

q: An iris coloboma is always located:
w: The fetal fissure lies on the inferior surface of the optic cup, so failure of closure always produces an inferior, keyhole-shaped defect.
k: Coloboma always inferior (6 o'clock fetal fissure).

q: A 58-year-old hypertensive patient wakes up with right-sided ptosis, right eye turned outward and downward, and pupil 7mm and non-reactive. What is the diagnosis and what does the pupil finding suggest?
w: Complete right CN3 palsy presents with: ptosis (LPS paralyzed), eye down and out (SR/IR/MR paralyzed; unopposed LR + SO), dilated non-reactive pupil (sphincter pupillae paralyzed). The KEY finding is the dilated, non-reactive pupil — parasympathetic pupillomotor fibers run on the outside surface of CN3. External compression (aneurysm of the posterior communicating artery) preferentially affects these outer fibers FIRST, causing early pupil involvement. Ischemic CN3 palsy (diabetes, hypertension) affects the inner motor fibers first while sparing the outer pupillomotor fibers. Pupil-involving CN3 palsy = surgical emergency until neuroimaging rules out aneurysm. CN6 palsy causes esotropia, not the down-and-out eye. CN4 palsy causes hypertropia/head tilt, not ptosis.
k: Complete right CN3 palsy presents with: ptosis (LPS paralyzed), eye down and out (SR/IR/MR paralyzed…

q: A child presents with preseptal eyelid swelling, proptosis, restricted eye movement, and fever. What is the most likely source of infection based on anatomy?
w: Orbital cellulitis in children most commonly originates from the ethmoidal sinus. The medial orbital wall (lamina papyracea of the ethmoid) is extremely thin and directly adjacent to the ethmoidal air cells. Bacterial sinusitis erodes through this wall, spreading infection into the orbit. The presentation — preseptal and postseptal involvement, proptosis, restricted EOM, fever — is the classic orbital cellulitis picture. The sphenoidal sinus is posterior to the orbit and relates to CN2 (retrobulbar neuritis), not direct orbital spread. The maxillary sinus relates to the orbital floor (blowout fractures). The frontal sinus relates to the orbital roof.
k: Orbital cellulitis in children most commonly originates from the ethmoidal sinus.

q: A 45-year-old patient presents with vertical diplopia that worsens when looking down-and-inward. He tilts his head toward his left shoulder when reading. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
w: CN4 (trochlear) innervates the superior oblique. The SO's primary action is intorsion, secondary is depression. When it fails, the eye cannot depress or intort — this causes vertical diplopia worse on downgaze and in adduction (looking down-inward). The patient compensates by tilting the head toward the opposite shoulder (here, left), which is the classic Bielschowsky head tilt. A CN3 palsy would cause a larger picture — ptosis, dilated pupil, and the eye would be down and out. CN6 palsy causes horizontal diplopia (esotropia), not vertical.
k: CN4 (trochlear) innervates the superior oblique.

q: Which of the following correctly lists the contents of the superior orbital fissure?
o: CN3, CN4, CN6, superior ophthalmic vein
CN2, CN3, CN4, CN6, ophthalmic artery
w: The SOF carries CN3 (oculomotor), CN4 (trochlear), CN5-ophthalmic division (via its branches: frontal, lacrimal, nasociliary), CN6 (abducens), and the superior ophthalmic vein. CN2 and the ophthalmic artery pass through the optic canal — not the SOF. The inferior orbital fissure carries CN5 maxillary division and the infraorbital artery.
k: The SOF carries CN3 (oculomotor), CN4 (trochlear), CN5-ophthalmic division (via its branches: frontal…

q: A 35-year-old patient complains of chronic eye irritation, foreign body sensation, and redness worsening in the afternoon. Schirmer's test (without anesthesia) measures 18 mm bilaterally. Tear break-up time is 5 seconds. Which of the following is the most likely underlying problem?
o: Excessive tear drainage due to punctal stenosis
Aqueous-deficient dry eye from lacrimal gland hyposecretion
Lipid layer deficiency from Meibomian gland dysfunction
w: The normal Schirmer's test (18 mm — well within 10–25 mm) rules out aqueous deficiency. The short TBUT (5 seconds, normal >10 seconds) indicates the tear film is breaking up too quickly despite adequate aqueous volume. The lipid layer (from Meibomian glands) is what prevents evaporation and maintains film stability. When it is deficient, the aqueous layer evaporates rapidly and the film collapses before the next blink. This is evaporative dry eye — the most common form. Symptoms worsen in the afternoon because Meibomian gland secretions accumulate and solidify with reduced blink-driven expression over the day.
k: The normal Schirmer's test (18 mm — well within 10–25 mm) rules out aqueous deficiency.

q: A 6-week-old infant is brought in with persistent unilateral epiphora and a mucopurulent discharge since birth. The most likely diagnosis is:
w: The nasolacrimal duct is the last segment of the lacrimal drainage system to fully open during development. In up to 20% of newborns, the valve of Hasner (at the nasal opening of the nasolacrimal duct) remains membranous at birth. Tears cannot drain, accumulate in the lacrimal sac, and become secondarily infected. This presents as persistent unilateral epiphora with mucopurulent discharge starting shortly after birth. Management is conservative (massage over lacrimal sac, topical antibiotics) — most resolve spontaneously by 12 months. Probing is performed if obstruction persists beyond that. Congenital glaucoma would present with photophobia, tearing, buphthalmos, and corneal edema — not mucopurulent discharge. Neonatal gonorrheal conjunctivitis presents in the first 2–5 days of life with severe bilateral purulent discharge, not epiphora.
k: The nasolacrimal duct is the last segment of the lacrimal drainage system to fully open during development.

q: A 70-year-old patient presents with progressive, painless bilateral vision loss. Slit lamp examination shows corneal edema with a beaten-metal appearance of the endothelium on specular reflection. Central pachymetry is 680 µm bilaterally. Which of the following best explains the pathophysiology?
o: Loss of endothelial Na-K ATPase pump function leading to stromal edema
Failure of goblet cells to produce mucin for the tear film
Degeneration of Bowman's membrane preventing epithelial adhesion
Disruption of tight junctions in the corneal epithelium
w: This is Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy. The endothelium normally pumps Na+ and HCO3- out of the stroma — water follows osmotically, keeping the stroma at 75–80% water. In Fuchs', endothelial cells degenerate and are not replaced (endothelium is post-mitotic). As cell density falls below a critical threshold, the pump cannot maintain deturgescence. Water accumulates in the stroma, disrupting the regular collagen fiber spacing and causing light scatter (opacity). Pachymetry above 600 µm (normal 520–560 µm) confirms stromal swelling. The "beaten-metal" or "hammered silver" appearance on specular microscopy is the endothelial guttata — excrescences on Descemet's membrane that are pathognomonic of Fuchs'. Goblet cells, tight junctions, and Bowman's membrane are unrelated to this presentation.
k: This is Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy.

q: During gonioscopy, the examiner identifies a thin, white, glistening line at the most anterior portion of the trabecular meshwork. What structure is this?
w: Schwalbe's line marks the peripheral termination of Descemet's membrane and the most anterior extension of the trabecular meshwork. It appears as a thin, white, glistening line projecting slightly into the anterior chamber. The gonioscopic order from anterior to posterior is: Schwalbe's line → trabecular meshwork → scleral spur → ciliary body band → peripheral iris. Sampaolesi's line is pigment deposited along Schwalbe's line — seen in pigment dispersion syndrome and pseudoexfoliation — and would appear as a pigmented line, not a white one. The scleral spur appears as a whitish band posterior to the TM. Schlemm's canal is not normally visible unless blood-filled.
k: Schwalbe's line marks the peripheral termination of Descemet's membrane and the most anterior extension of…

q: A patient is asked to look at a near target. Both pupils constrict. When a bright light is shone into the right eye, neither pupil constricts, but both constrict with near gaze. The left eye shows the same pattern. Which of the following best describes this condition?
w: Argyll-Robertson pupils are pathognomonic for neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis). The hallmark is light-near dissociation: "accommodates but does not react to light." The pretectal lesion disrupts the afferent arc of the pupillary light reflex while sparing the accommodation-convergence near reflex pathway (which travels more ventrally in the brainstem). This produces bilateral small, irregular pupils that constrict with near gaze but not with light. Bilateral Adie's is possible but would show tonic (slow) near constriction and loss of deep tendon reflexes, not the complete absence of light response. Marcus-Gunn involves a relative afferent defect — it does not cause bilateral failure of the light reflex in both eyes.
k: Argyll-Robertson pupils are pathognomonic for neurosyphilis (tabes dorsalis).

q: During a vitreoretinal surgical procedure, the surgeon enters the eye through which of the following structures to minimize bleeding risk?
w: The pars plana is the posterior, flat, avascular portion of the ciliary body (4mm wide, extending from the ora serrata to the pars plicata). Its relative avascularity makes it the preferred and safest entry point for vitreoretinal surgery, reducing hemorrhage risk. The pars plicata (anterior 2mm, most vascular) contains the ciliary processes and is avoided. The limbus is used for anterior segment surgeries (e.g., cataract). The iris root is fragile and its incision would damage the angle and drainage structures.
k: The pars plana is the posterior, flat, avascular portion of the ciliary body (4mm wide, extending from the…

q: A 55-year-old patient has had increasing difficulty reading. Their distance vision remains 20/20. Amplitude of accommodation measures +0.5D. Which of the following is the primary mechanism?
o: Ciliary muscle atrophy reducing zonular tension
Progressive hardening of the lens nucleus reducing elastic recoil
Posterior displacement of the lens reducing its refractive power
Zonule degeneration preventing transmission of ciliary muscle force
w: Presbyopia develops because the lens adds new fibers throughout life, compressing older fibers centrally into an increasingly hard nucleus. The nucleus loses its elasticity. Even when the ciliary muscle contracts and releases zonular tension, the stiff nucleus cannot round up sufficiently to increase power for near focus. The ciliary muscle itself remains functional in early presbyopia — the problem is purely in the lens. Zonule degeneration does not typically cause presbyopia; subluxation (e.g., Marfan's) causes different problems. Posterior lens displacement would reduce accommodation range but is not the primary mechanism of presbyopia.
k: Presbyopia develops because the lens adds new fibers throughout life, compressing older fibers centrally into…

q: Which of the following most accurately describes the composition of aqueous humor relative to plasma?
o: Higher ascorbic acid concentration than plasma
w: Aqueous humor is actively secreted — not just a filtrate of plasma — so certain components are concentrated above plasma levels. Ascorbic acid (vitamin C) is actively pumped into the AH at concentrations 15–20× higher than plasma. It serves as an antioxidant protecting the lens and cornea from oxidative damage. Protein content is very low (25 mg% vs. Plasma ~7000 mg%) — the blood-aqueous barrier restricts protein entry, keeping AH optically clear. Osmolarity is slightly higher than plasma (not lower). Glucose is present at about 2.5 mg% in AH — lower than plasma.
k: Aqueous humor is actively secreted — not just a filtrate of plasma — so certain components are concentrated…

q: A patient with advanced glaucoma retains central vision but has complete loss of peripheral visual field. Which retinal cells are most directly responsible for the peripheral field loss?
o: RPE cells at the ora serrata
Bipolar cells at the inner nuclear layer
Peripheral rods and their associated ganglion cells
w: Glaucoma damages retinal ganglion cells — specifically their axons at the lamina cribrosa where elevated IOP compresses them. The peripheral ganglion cells (associated with peripheral rods for peripheral field) are more vulnerable and lost earlier in glaucoma. This produces the classic arcuate scotoma and nasal step pattern, eventually leaving only a central island of vision (tunnel vision from surviving foveal cone-ganglion pathways). The foveal cones themselves are among the last to be affected because the foveal area has a relative abundance of ganglion cells and better vascular supply. RPE cells and bipolar cells are not the primary site of glaucomatous damage.
k: Glaucoma damages retinal ganglion cells — specifically their axons at the lamina cribrosa where elevated IOP…

q: On fundus examination, the optic disc measures 1.5mm and the cup measures 0.7mm in diameter. Which of the following is most accurate?
o: C/D ratio of 0.47 — borderline suspicious for glaucoma
C/D ratio of 0.7 — very concerning for glaucomatous cupping
w: C/D ratio = cup diameter ÷ disc diameter = 0.7 ÷ 1.5 = 0.47. Normal C/D is ≤0.3. A ratio of 0.47 is above normal and warrants further evaluation — IOP measurement, visual field testing, and OCT of the nerve fiber layer. It is suspicious but not necessarily diagnostic.7 would be the cup diameter, not the ratio). A C/D >0.5 with asymmetry >0.2 between the two eyes raises high concern for glaucoma.
k: C/D ratio = cup diameter ÷ disc diameter = 0.7 ÷ 1.5 = 0.47.

q: A patient presents with sudden painless vision loss, a pale swollen retina, and a cherry-red spot at the fovea. Which vascular event explains the cherry-red spot?
o: Inner retinal edema sparing the thin foveola
Foveal hemorrhage from a vein occlusion
w: In central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO), the inner retina (supplied by the CRA) becomes ischemic and edematous — the swollen cells make the retina appear pale and opaque. However, the fovea (specifically the foveola) is extremely thin — it lacks ganglion cells and inner nuclear cells. At the foveola, the transparent inner retina is so thin that the underlying choroid (still perfused by the short posterior ciliary arteries, which are not affected by CRAO) shows through as a red color against the pale surrounding retina. This contrast creates the cherry-red spot. RPE detachment is a different pathology. Vein occlusion causes flame hemorrhages, not cherry-red spot.
k: In central retinal artery occlusion (CRAO), the inner retina (supplied by the CRA) becomes ischemic and…

q: A 45-year-old woman presents with gradual loss of her peripheral vision bilaterally. She bumps into objects on both sides. Visual field testing shows loss of both temporal fields while nasal fields are intact. MRI would most likely reveal a lesion at which location?
w: Bitemporal hemianopsia — loss of both temporal fields with preserved nasal fields — is the hallmark of a chiasmal lesion affecting the crossing (decussating) fibers. These nasal fibers carry temporal field information from both eyes; their interruption at the chiasma eliminates both temporal fields simultaneously. The most common cause in a middle-aged woman is a pituitary adenoma growing upward to compress the inferior chiasma. A right optic nerve lesion would cause right monocular blindness. A right optic tract lesion would cause left homonymous hemianopsia (not bitemporal). Occipital cortex lesion causes homonymous scotoma — not bitemporal loss.
k: Bitemporal hemianopsia — loss of both temporal fields with preserved nasal fields — is the hallmark of a…

q: A 60-year-old man suffers a right-sided stroke involving the temporal lobe. Which visual field defect is most likely on examination?
o: Left homonymous hemianopsia with macular sparing
w: The inferior fibers of the right optic radiation loop through the temporal lobe (Meyer's loop) before reaching the occipital cortex. These fibers carry information from the superior visual field of the left hemifield. A right temporal lobe stroke interrupts Meyer's loop, producing a left superior quadrantanopsia — classically called "pie in the sky" (the upper-left visual field is lost). Left inferior quadrantanopsia would result from a right parietal lobe lesion (superior radiation fibers). Left homonymous hemianopsia with macular sparing is a right occipital cortex finding. Bitemporal hemianopsia requires a chiasmal lesion.
k: The inferior fibers of the right optic radiation loop through the temporal lobe (Meyer's loop) before…

q: A newborn presents with leukocoria (white pupil). On examination, a white fibrovascular mass is noted behind the lens. B-scan ultrasound shows a retrolental mass with no calcium deposits. Which embryological failure best explains this finding?
o: Failure of secondary vitreous to form
Failure of primary vitreous to regress
Failure of lens vesicle to separate from surface ectoderm
w: This is Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV), now called Persistent Fetal Vasculature (PFV). The primary vitreous, which is vascular and contains the hyaloid artery system, normally regresses completely by birth. When it persists, the remaining fibrovascular tissue forms a white retrolental mass — leukocoria. The absence of calcium deposits distinguishes it from retinoblastoma (which often calcifies). Fetal fissure failure produces coloboma (not a retrolental mass). Lens vesicle failure would prevent lens formation. Secondary vitreous failure does not cause leukocoria.
k: This is Persistent Hyperplastic Primary Vitreous (PHPV), now called Persistent Fetal Vasculature (PFV).

q: A 16-year-old male with tall stature, long fingers, and arachnodactyly presents with sudden visual blur in the right eye. Slit-lamp exam shows the right lens displaced superiorly. Which of the following correctly links the genetic defect to the embryological structure affected?
o: FBN1 mutation → weak fibrillin zonules → lens dislocation
PAX6 mutation → failure of iris differentiation → aniridia
COL4A1 mutation → abnormal lens capsule → lens rupture
FBN1 mutation → weak secondary vitreous → lens dislocation
w: This is Marfan syndrome. The tertiary vitreous forms the lens zonules (suspensory ligament) from fibrillin-containing microfibrils. FBN1 encodes fibrillin-1. When fibrillin is structurally abnormal, the zonules are weak and break under normal tension. The lens dislocates — classically in the superotemporal direction. This is ectopia lentis. The secondary vitreous forms the vitreous gel, not the zonules. PAX6 mutation causes aniridia — no lens dislocation. COL4A1 mutations affect type IV collagen and cause cerebrovascular disease, not lens dislocation.
k: This is Marfan syndrome.

q: The lipid layer of the tear film comes mainly from the:
w: Meibomian glands supply most of the lipid, with a contribution from the glands of Zeis. Losing it gives evaporative dry eye: normal Schirmer, short TBUT.
k: Lipid layer = Meibomian (plus Zeis).

q: Reflex aqueous tear secretion comes from the:
w: The main lacrimal gland handles reflex tearing under CN7 control. Basic resting secretion comes from the accessory glands of Krause and Wolfring.
k: Reflex = main lacrimal gland (CN7); basic = Krause + Wolfring.

q: The thinnest layer of the tear film is the:
w: Mucin is thinnest at about 0.03 µm, lipid next at 0.5 µm, and aqueous thickest at 7 µm. Thin does not mean unimportant: mucin is what lets the aqueous wet the cornea at all.
k: Mucin 0.03 µm < lipid 0.5 µm < aqueous 7 µm.

q: CN7 reaches the lacrimal gland by which route?
w: The parasympathetic path runs greater petrosal → Vidian → pterygopalatine ganglion → lacrimal nerve. Misdirected regeneration along it produces crocodile tears.
k: CN7 → greater petrosal → Vidian → pterygopalatine → lacrimal.

q: A normal Schirmer's test without anesthesia measures:
w: Normal is 10–25 mm in 5 minutes without anesthesia. Under 10 mm suggests aqueous deficiency. TBUT under 10 seconds points instead to instability.
k: Schirmer normal 10–25 mm/5 min; TBUT >10 s.

q: Lysozyme makes up roughly what share of tear protein?
w: Lysozyme is 21–25% of tear protein and lyses bacterial cell walls, working alongside secretory IgA and lactoferrin as the tear film's defense.
k: Lysozyme = 21–25% of tear protein.

q: The six corneal layers from anterior to posterior are:
o: Endothelium → Descemet's → Dua's → stroma → Bowman's → epithelium
Epithelium → stroma → Bowman's → Dua's → Descemet's → endothelium
Epithelium → Bowman's → stroma → Dua's → Descemet's → endothelium
Epithelium → Bowman's → Dua's → stroma → Descemet's → endothelium
w: Epithelium, Bowman's, stroma, Dua's, Descemet's, endothelium. Dua's layer is the modern addition, a tough pre-Descemet's layer relevant to deep lamellar grafting.
k: Epi → Bowman's → stroma → Dua's → Descemet's → endothelium.

q: Which corneal layers CAN regenerate?
w: Epithelium regenerates from limbal stem cells, and the endothelium secretes new Descemet's. Bowman's and the endothelium itself cannot regenerate, which is why their loss is permanent.
k: Regenerate: epithelium, Descemet's. Never: Bowman's, endothelium.

q: The limbus is how wide, and what does it contain?
w: The limbus is a 1–2 mm transition zone holding the trabecular meshwork, Schlemm's canal and the limbal stem cells that resurface the epithelium.
k: Limbus 1–2 mm: TM, Schlemm's, stem cells.

q: The gonioscopic landmarks from anterior to posterior are:
o: Schwalbe's → TM → scleral spur → ciliary body → iris
Scleral spur → Schwalbe's → TM → ciliary body → iris
TM → Schwalbe's → scleral spur → iris → ciliary body
Ciliary body → iris → scleral spur → TM → Schwalbe's
w: Anterior to posterior: Schwalbe's line, trabecular meshwork, scleral spur, ciliary body band, peripheral iris. Identifying the scleral spur is what tells you the angle is open.
k: Gonioscopy: Schwalbe's → TM → spur → ciliary body → iris.

q: The foveola measures about:
w: The foveola is 0.4 mm of pure cones and is capillary-free, sitting within the 1.5 mm fovea. Being avascular, it survives a CRAO on choroidal supply, giving the cherry-red spot.
k: Foveola 0.4 mm (pure cones, avascular); fovea 1.5 mm.

q: The ora serrata lies approximately how far from the limbus?
w: The ora serrata sits about 6 mm behind the limbus, marking the anterior termination of the retina. Scleral depression is needed to view it.
k: Ora serrata ≈ 6 mm from the limbus.

q: The macular xanthophyll pigments are:
w: Lutein and zeaxanthin filter blue light and mop up free radicals at the macula. They are the rationale behind the AREDS2 formulation.
k: Macular pigment = lutein + zeaxanthin (AREDS2).

q: Counting from outer to inner, which is the OUTERMOST retinal layer?
w: The order runs RPE, photoreceptors, ELM, ONL, Henle's, OPL, INL, IPL, GCL, NFL, ILM. Light traverses the whole thickness to reach the photoreceptors: the retina is inverted.
k: Outer to inner: RPE → photoreceptors → … → NFL → ILM.

q: The optic disc sits how far above the foveola?
w: The disc lies about 3 mm nasal to the macula and 0.8 mm above the foveola, which is why the physiologic blind spot maps slightly below the horizontal midline.
k: Disc: 3 mm nasal, 0.8 mm above the foveola.

q: The fetal fissure normally closes at which week?
w: The fetal fissure closes at week 7, the 14 mm stage. Failure leaves an inferior coloboma at 6 o'clock, since the fissure runs along the underside of the optic cup.
k: Fetal fissure closes week 7 (14 mm stage).

q: The hyaloid artery has fully regressed by:
w: The hyaloid artery regresses by about week 36. Failure leaves PHPV with leukocoria; benign remnants include Cloquet's canal and a Mittendorf dot on the posterior lens capsule.
k: Hyaloid artery gone by week 36/birth.

q: A Mittendorf dot on the posterior lens capsule is:
w: A Mittendorf dot is a harmless remnant of the hyaloid artery's attachment to the posterior lens. It needs no treatment and must not be mistaken for pathology.
k: Mittendorf dot = benign hyaloid remnant.

q: The extraocular muscles derive from:
w: The EOMs are mesodermal. The lens and lacrimal gland come from surface ectoderm, the retina from neuroectoderm, and the corneal stroma and endothelium from neural crest.
k: EOMs = mesoderm.

q: The lacrimal gland derives from:
w: The lacrimal gland is surface ectoderm, the same origin as the lens and the corneal epithelium.
k: Lacrimal gland = surface ectoderm.

q: Aniridia results from a mutation in which gene?

w: Two long posterior ciliary arteries travel in the perichoroidal space and combine with seven anterior ciliary arteries (which run with the rectus muscles) to form the greater arterial circle of the iris.
k: Greater iris circle = 2 LPCA + 7 ACA.

q: The choroid is supplied by which arteries?
w: The short posterior ciliary arteries (10-20 branches entering in a ring around the optic nerve) supply the choroid. The long posterior ciliaries serve the iris and ciliary body instead.
k: Short PCAs = choroid; long PCAs = iris/ciliary body.

q: The lateral muscular branch of the ophthalmic artery supplies which group?
w: The lateral muscular branch serves the lateral rectus, superior rectus, superior oblique and levator; the medial branch serves the inferior rectus, medial rectus and inferior oblique.
k: Lateral branch: LR, SR, SO, LPS. Medial: IR, MR, IO.

q: The lacrimal artery, besides the lacrimal gland, also partly supplies which muscle?
w: The lacrimal artery contributes to the lateral rectus supply on its way to the gland, which is why it appears in both the vascular and the muscular lists.
k: Lacrimal artery: lacrimal gland + partial LR.
